So once we have confirmed that all bits in k are present in n, then the number of changes is the number of bits that are set in (n XOR k), divided by 2? Or not? Wait, n XOR k gives bits that are different between the two. For example, in example 1, n is 13 (1101), k is 4 (0100). XOR is 1101 XOR 0100 → 1001. So the algorithm is:

Check if (n & k) !=k → return -1.

Else, count the number of set bits in (n ^ k).

So how to implement this in Java.

Now, for Java, the code would be:

public int minChanges(int n, int k) {
    if ((n & k) != k) {
        return -1;
    }
    int xor = n ^ k;
    return Integer.bitCount(xor);
}

So the code would be:

class Solution {
    public int minChanges(int n, int k) {
        if ((n & k) != k) {
            return -1;
        }
        int xor = n ^ k;
        return Integer.bitCount(xor);
    }
}

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public int minChanges(int n, int k) {
        if ((n & k) != k) {
            return -1;
        }
        return Integer.bitCount(n ^ k);
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(1)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution checks if k is a subset of n's bits using bitwise AND. If not possible, returns -1. Otherwise, counts differing bits (bits to flip) using XOR and bitCount. Efficient with constant time and space.
